title: From Creative Chaos to Intentional Leadership: Lindsay Buwalda's Journey
guestBio: Lindsay Buwalda is the founder of Bliss Productions and BP Collective, a visionary event designer and branding expert known for crafting immersive weddings, corporate experiences, and transformational retreats. As a mother of two and creative powerhouse, she helps clients create meaningful brand experiences while navigating her own journey of entrepreneurship and leadership.
excerpt: Creative entrepreneur Lindsay Buwalda opens up about the invisible labor women carry, overcoming being underestimated in business, and how she's building her empire while balancing motherhood. This conversation will remind you that your creativity can indeed change lives.
metaDescription: Lindsay Buwalda shares how she built Bliss Productions while navigating invisible labor, patriarchal barriers, and motherhood as a creative leader.
keyTakeaways: The invisible labor women carry extends far beyond household tasks into emotional and logistical work that often goes unrecognized.
Being underestimated as a woman in business can actually become a strategic advantage when you learn to leverage it.
Technology can be a powerful equalizer for creative entrepreneurs juggling multiple roles and responsibilities.
Personal influence grows when you embrace intentional design in both business and life decisions.
Creative leadership requires courage to rise above traditional expectations and forge your own path.
Balancing motherhood and entrepreneurship means accepting that perfection isn't the goal—progress is.
Building a sustainable creative business requires setting clear boundaries and protecting your energy for what matters most.

pullQuotes.text: Your work matters, your leadership counts, and your creativity can change lives.

timestamps.label: Building Bliss Productions and BP Collective

content:
There's something profound that happens when two powerhouse women sit down to have an honest conversation about the reality of building businesses while managing families, creative visions, and the countless invisible tasks that keep life moving forward. In this episode of The Manage Her, Aimee reconnects with longtime friend Lindsay Buwalda, and what unfolds is a masterclass in creative leadership, resilience, and the art of turning challenges into competitive advantages.

Lindsay Buwalda isn't just another event planner or branding expert. As the founder of Bliss Productions and BP Collective, she's built her reputation on creating immersive experiences that transform how people connect with brands and each other. But like so many women leaders, her journey hasn't been a straight line from vision to success. It's been a winding path of reinvention, filled with moments of doubt, breakthrough insights, and the constant balancing act that defines modern womanhood.

The Weight of What We Don't See

One of the most powerful themes that emerges from this conversation is the concept of invisible labor—that mental and emotional workload that women carry without recognition or compensation. Lindsay opens up about how this extends far beyond remembering to buy milk or scheduling doctor's appointments. It's the emotional regulation, the relationship maintenance, the logistical coordination that keeps families and businesses running smoothly while the woman doing it all remains largely unseen.

This invisible labor becomes even more complex when you're running a creative business. You're not just managing your own creative output and client relationships; you're often carrying the emotional weight of making other people's most important moments perfect. Whether it's a wedding that represents years of dreams or a corporate retreat designed to transform team dynamics, the pressure to deliver transcendent experiences while managing your own life can feel overwhelming.

But here's what Lindsay has learned: acknowledging this labor is the first step to redistributing it. You can't manage what you don't measure, and you can't delegate what you don't first recognize as work. When you start naming the invisible tasks, you can begin to automate, delegate, or eliminate them strategically.

Turning Underestimation Into Advantage

Throughout her career, Lindsay has faced the familiar challenge of being underestimated—by clients who assume creativity means chaos, by vendors who think they can take advantage of a woman-owned business, by anyone who mistakes kindness for weakness. But rather than letting these experiences diminish her, she's learned to weaponize them in the most elegant way possible.

When people underestimate you, they often reveal more about their strategies, budgets, and vulnerabilities than they would to someone they perceived as a threat. They speak more freely, negotiate less carefully, and sometimes hand you advantages without realizing it. Lindsay has learned to use this dynamic to gather information, build stronger client relationships, and position herself as the solution to problems others didn't even know they had.

The key is maintaining your confidence internally while allowing others to maintain their misconceptions until the moment you choose to reveal your full capabilities. It's a delicate dance that requires emotional intelligence, strategic thinking, and unshakeable self-worth.

Technology as the Great Equalizer

One of the most practical aspects of this conversation centers on how technology can level the playing field for creative entrepreneurs juggling multiple roles. Lindsay shares her favorite tools and systems that allow her to maintain creative excellence while managing the business side of her operations efficiently.

The right technology stack can give a small creative business the capabilities of a much larger operation. Project management platforms keep complex events organized, automated scheduling systems reduce back-and-forth communication, and design software allows for rapid iteration and client collaboration. But more than efficiency, these tools provide something precious: mental bandwidth for creativity.

When you're not constantly worried about whether you forgot to follow up with a vendor or whether the client received the latest design mockup, you can focus on what you do best—creating experiences that matter. Technology doesn't replace creativity; it creates space for creativity to flourish.

The Evolution of Influence

Perhaps the most inspiring part of Lindsay's journey is watching her step more fully into her role as a leader and influencer in the creative industry. Through BP Collective, she's not just creating individual events or brand experiences; she's building a community and platform that elevates other creative professionals.

This evolution from service provider to industry leader requires a fundamental shift in how you see your role. Instead of just executing other people's visions, you start shaping the conversation about what's possible. You become someone who doesn't just respond to trends but creates them.

Lindsay's approach to this transition offers a roadmap for any creative professional ready to expand their influence. It starts with consistently delivering exceptional work that speaks for itself. Then it involves sharing insights and perspectives that help others in your industry. Finally, it means building platforms and creating opportunities that benefit the entire creative ecosystem, not just your own business.

Looking Forward

As this conversation reveals, Lindsay's story is still being written. The future holds expanded opportunities for Bliss Productions and BP Collective, new ways to serve clients, and continued growth in her leadership within the creative industry. But what's most striking is how she's approaching this future—not as someone trying to prove herself, but as someone who knows her worth and is ready to share her gifts with the world.

For any woman listening who wears multiple hats—CEO and caregiver, creator and coordinator—Lindsay's journey offers both permission and practical wisdom. Permission to acknowledge the full scope of what you manage, to claim your space as a leader, and to trust that your creative vision has the power to change lives. And practical wisdom about how technology, boundaries, and strategic thinking can help you build something sustainable and meaningful.

Your creativity isn't a side project or a hobby that fits around your other responsibilities. It's a force for change, a source of influence, and a path to building the life and business you envision. Lindsay Buwalda's story proves that with intention, resilience, and the right support systems, you can transform creative chaos into purposeful leadership that serves both your vision and your values.

quiz.title: What's Your Creative Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally navigate the balance between creativity, leadership, and life's invisible demands. Your results will reveal your unique approach to building something meaningful while managing it all.

questions.question: When you're feeling overwhelmed by everything on your plate, what's your go-to response?

options.text: I step back and trust my instincts about what really matters right now
I create a system or list to organize everything into manageable pieces
I focus on one concrete task I can complete to build momentum
I reach out to someone I trust to talk through what I'm feeling

questions.question: How do you typically handle it when others underestimate your capabilities?

options.text: I let my unconventional results speak for themselves
I calmly demonstrate my competence through strategic actions
I use it as fuel to work even harder and prove them wrong
I focus on building relationships with people who see my value

questions.question: What aspect of invisible labor do you find most draining?

options.text: Having to explain or justify my creative vision constantly
Managing all the details that others don't even notice need doing
Carrying the mental load of everyone else's needs and deadlines
Being the emotional regulator for everyone around me

questions.question: When making important decisions, you tend to:

options.text: Follow what feels right, even if I can't fully explain why
Consider all the variables and create a plan with clear steps
Look at what's worked before and adapt it to the current situation
Think about how the decision will impact everyone involved

questions.question: Your biggest leadership challenge is probably:

options.text: Getting others to buy into ideas that feel risky or different
Maintaining boundaries while still being helpful and available
Fighting perfectionism and accepting that good enough is sometimes okay
Asking for help instead of trying to handle everything myself

questions.question: What energizes you most about your work or projects?

options.text: The moment when a wild idea starts becoming reality
Seeing all the pieces come together exactly as planned
Overcoming obstacles that would stop most people
Creating something that brings people together or transforms their experience

results.title: The Intuitive Innovator
results.description: You lead with your gut and aren't afraid to break the mold when something feels right. Your creative vision drives everything you do, and you're naturally drawn to opportunities that others might overlook. Sometimes you feel misunderstood, but that's often your greatest strength—you see possibilities where others see obstacles. Lindsay's journey from creative chaos to intentional leadership will resonate deeply with your experience of turning unconventional thinking into competitive advantage.

results.title: The Strategic Harmonizer
results.description: You excel at seeing the big picture while keeping all the moving pieces in perfect balance. People rely on you because you naturally anticipate what needs to happen and when. Your superpower lies in creating systems that allow creativity to flourish within structure. You understand that sustainable success requires both vision and boundaries. This episode will speak to your desire to lead intentionally while managing the invisible labor that keeps everything running smoothly.

results.title: The Resilient Builder
results.description: You're the person who gets things done, no matter what obstacles appear along the way. Your leadership style is grounded in persistence and practical problem-solving, and you've learned to turn challenges into stepping stones. You understand that progress matters more than perfection, and you're not afraid to pivot when needed. Lindsay's honest conversation about balancing motherhood, entrepreneurship, and the reality of building something sustainable will validate your own journey of steady growth through life's complexities.

results.title: The Empowered Connector
results.description: Your leadership strength comes from your ability to see and elevate others while building meaningful relationships. You understand that success isn't just about your own achievements—it's about creating experiences and opportunities that transform everyone involved. You're naturally collaborative and have an intuitive sense of what people need to thrive. This episode will inspire you to embrace how your relational intelligence and ability to manage invisible emotional labor can become powerful business assets.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Welcome back to The Manage Her, where we explore the inspiring journeys of women navigating life, work, and family with strength and grace. I'm your host, Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manage Her: Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, now available on Amazon. 

Today I'm deeply honored to introduce Lindsay Buwalda, the visionary founder of Bliss Productions and BP Collective based in our shared hometown of Laguna Beach, California. Lindsay is an extraordinary event designer and brand expert with over 20 years of experience crafting unforgettable weddings, corporate events, and retreats. A mother of two, she brings creativity and intention to everything she does. Fun fact, we graduated from Laguna Beach High School together, and it's a joy to reconnect.

Lindsay, you've read chapter one of The Manage Her. To start, could you take me through a day in the life of busy mom and entrepreneur? What does that look like for you?

**Lindsay:** Busy mom and entrepreneur. Wow. Well, it's different every day. As you know, kids wake up. I wake up at 4:35 usually and then I try to meditate right when I wake up and maybe try to do a cold plunge. And then after that, I'll start to work, get the kids ready to go to school and try to make it super joyful at that time. 

And then after I drop them off, I'm a runner. I'm still a runner after all these years. Still running. So, I'll go on a run usually by the beach or in the hills and then that resets my mind and gets me started for the day and then I'll work until I pick them up from school.

**Aimee:** Nice. Yeah. So, what are your working hours like? What's your time block?

**Lindsay:** Yeah. So, time is super essential, as you know, being a mom and trying to juggle everything, shuffle everything. But I'll usually do an hour and a half time block in the morning before they wake up and then from like 8, well, I run. So, then from about 9 to 3 and then now they have sports. So, if they do, then I'll do a little bit more. 

That's awesome. And what I've been trying to do is I think everybody has a little bit of ADD. So, I'll literally do like 25 minute increments and I'll time it and then I'll give myself 3 minutes in between to do something fun.

**Aimee:** Oh, I love that. What do you do in your fun time? What's your 3-minute fun?

**Lindsay:** Well, I guess it's not that fun. Maybe I'll do the dishes or it gives me a break. And then I'll get competitive with myself. So, I'll try to, like, you have to finish this task in 25 minutes. But, it's really helpful.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I love that. Because it doesn't go on and on. Yeah. I love that. I love time blocking, too. Because if I know I only have to do something for a certain amount of time, I'm actually way more productive than if I had all the time in the day to do something. I absolutely think this is why mothers are so productive because we have a lot to do and we don't have a lot of time to do it in. So, it's be intentional.

**Lindsay:** Be intentional. I love it. Yes. Exactly. Intentional with your time.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. I love that. That's definitely one of my things for sure. What's one invisible labor task that you manage that keeps your family going?

**Lindsay:** Invisible labor task. Well, there's a lot, but one of them is just making sure that you tap into their emotions, right? And that they're doing okay and that you guys are in sync and on the same page and that they're happy. There's only so many years before they leave. And so that's something that I think is imperative.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. Absolutely. And invisible.

**Lindsay:** Invisible. It's so invisible.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's so invisible, but so essential, isn't it?

**Lindsay:** I know. And I don't want to gloss over that. And then I think when they're happy and doing okay and you feel like you're in sync with them, then I think the family is in a better spot.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. I can only be as happy as my saddest child.

**Lindsay:** It's so true. I think my mom said that to me.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh, that's a good one. She's got eight kids.

**Lindsay:** I know. And she would that was her thing and I'm like, "Oh my gosh, how hard is that with eight kids?" But that is amazing. It's true. You got six kids and if you have one that's not doing so happy, you're like, "Oh, okay. I feel that."

**Aimee:** Totally. Yeah. And I want them to be able to be vocal and express their feelings, right? I think that's super important, right?

**Lindsay:** Absolutely. Yeah. I love that.

**Aimee:** In chapter one, we talk about the patriarchal challenges of living in this world, right? Have you come across being underestimated as a woman or experienced this in your career in event designing or any of that thing?

**Lindsay:** I think anybody when you first start in any industry, of course, you're going to experience that and that was okay at the beginning because maybe I should have been underestimated a little bit, right? But then as you grow and become more confident, I think in what you're doing and in your career then and as a woman and as you evolve and get to know yourself more, I think that you kind of maybe softly demand that confidence and then you're not overlooked. 

So for me, yes, absolutely. Especially I worked in the corporate world. So I did marketing and branding for Ford and First American. So it was very male-dominated. So I think that yeah I was dismissed a little bit but I think I grew a lot from that and learned and now owning my own business I think just having those boundaries too is important.

**Aimee:** So that's a long answer to your question. No, I love that. Yeah. I think we've all had a little taste of it along the way especially when we were young and pretty.

**Lindsay:** Yes. Yes. Yes. Absolutely. I worked with a lot of dealers at Ford from the dealerships. So that was that was interesting.

**Aimee:** I bet. I can only imagine.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. And it was a different time. It was, you know, 20 years ago. 20 plus years ago.

**Aimee:** Yes. Absolutely. Yeah. Right. You do. You learn. You learn and you grow from it. For sure.

Could you share a moment when you felt like your expertise was overlooked and how did you overcome it and reaffirm your leadership?

**Lindsay:** That's such a good question. I think owning my own company, I've had Bliss Productions for 15 years. But when I was first starting, going back to boundaries, I think that, you know, you would do anything for any client and I still would, but in a very different way. 

So now I'm more structured in terms of what times they can contact me and how they can contact me. And it's, you know, I think a lot of times people will look at maybe what you're doing as a hobby, but it's how I make my living. I think I'm more direct and more structured and I push back a lot more than I used to.

**Aimee:** I love that. Yeah. I think that's one of the big things that you learn from being in business is how to have boundaries around your personal time and not work all the time because you'll really burn yourself out.

**Lindsay:** Oh, burnout is a thing.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. Did you find that when you first got started that you were pushing yourself too hard?

**Lindsay:** Yes. And I mean even now and especially now starting BP Collective as well, it's like there you have to. Well, then you just have to bring people in and be okay with that too. There's that balance.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Absolutely. Oh, bringing in employees that you can trust.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. Absolutely. The right ones, right?

**Aimee:** Well, that's always the tricky part, isn't it?

**Lindsay:** It can be. Yeah. Yeah. Absolutely. And then also communicating with those what you need from your employees clearly so that they know how to perform.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. And how to help you, right?

**Lindsay:** Yeah. Yeah. And then implementing those structures within your own companies so that it can start running by itself a little bit more and better.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. I feel like this incarnation of my life, my companies, the companies that we have going right now are so much more on autopilot.

**Lindsay:** Yes. And I love that.

**Aimee:** Good for you. We used to work our butts off. Like for real, we worked, you know, seven days a week. Even on holidays we'd be working with clients and it was non-stop. And that's not healthy.

**Lindsay:** No, no, no. It's not. No. It's the recipe for burnout.

**Aimee:** Exactly. So nice to have that shift, too, when you can actually shift it and then it starts running itself.

**Lindsay:** Oh, it's so nice. Yeah. We become more systems-oriented, right? As you become a better entrepreneur.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. I know. Right. It's hard to learn too, I think. And even how to do that. And sometimes you have to bring people in to help you with that. A lot of times if you bring in key employees that really can self-manage.

**Lindsay:** Yes. That's when you're grooving.

**Aimee:** I know. I know. It's all about your team. It really is.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. You're only as good as your team.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. I'm glad. Do you have a good team right now?

**Lindsay:** I do. Oh, that's fantastic. I know. I really do. Yes. I'm sure you do as well.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. Yeah. Our team is on point at Tomahawk especially. I'm just so proud of my guys. They are really doing great things. And it's so nice that, you know, we have such reliable employees that really feel like partners to us in our companies who really own their part of the business. And I'd say the same thing at Tomahawk, too. I mean, we have really good people around us. And I think that's the key.

**Lindsay:** That is the key. I know. And you want them to stay and you want them to own it. Be a part of it. Be a part of this thing and grow this together because it's when one person succeeds in a team, everyone succeeds.

**Aimee:** Everybody does know and I always want them to grow. I want them to feel that ownership and because they are a part of it.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. Yeah. And with that if you have an employee with that ownership mentality then all of a sudden it's really that's when you can have exponential growth in your company.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. It's not just a job.

**Lindsay:** Yes. Yes. There's a big difference. Yeah. Having somebody who can find that passion for it that you have.

**Aimee:** I know. I know. And sometimes that's hard to find because you don't know until they get started.

**Lindsay:** Yes. Yes. Or at least that's been my experience. A lot of times you're just guessing.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Even though you think that you did a good interview, sometimes they just have to do it.

**Lindsay:** Totally. And you never know. You never know.

**Aimee:** Yeah. We started Tomahawk, you know, we made the pivot to the commercial world from the government space in 2020. So, we hired the VP of commercial sales March 13th of 2020, right before the world shut down. Literally the day before the world shut down for COVID. So, it was a Hail Mary not knowing whether, you know, we had made the right choice, you know, but man, did we make the right choice.

**Lindsay:** Thank goodness. Yeah. That's awesome.

**Aimee:** It was the best choice. It was so weird, wasn't it? How was running your business through COVID? I mean, we do events.

**Lindsay:** Yeah, it was so nuts. We just paused a lot and then I mean, I'm not a huge rule follower, so we still did a lot of events just in a different way, right? And maybe less and then there was the space and all that stuff, but yeah, and it shifted pretty quickly.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Well, things still had to be done. People were still going to get married.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. There were still events to be had. There were still, just in a different form.

**Aimee:** Yeah. So, you just had to pivot a little bit.

**Lindsay:** Exactly. Which is life, right?

**Aimee:** Yeah. Totally. Adjust. We say adjust and conquer AC.

**Lindsay:** Exactly. I like that.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. We did it. Tomahawk, our first deal in the commercial space for Tomahawk. Instead of selling it, we sold a train car full of hand sanitizer.

**Lindsay:** Perfect.

**Aimee:** To one of the large train companies. Oh my gosh, I love it. And we found a spice manufacturer here in Orange County and they actually made hand sanitizer for us.

**Lindsay:** Amazing. See a spice. So everybody had to pivot a little bit, but you just do what you need to do. And sometimes it's an opportunity that you would have never thought of, right?

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. It cracked me up. I thought, "Oh my gosh, I can't believe we're doing this right now. This is hilarious." But we did what they needed. That's what they needed. And so we made it happen.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. And it's more fun that way, too.

**Aimee:** It really is. And I think a lot of people are so scared to just do it, right? To just roll up your sleeves and whatever that is to just figure it out.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. You got to roll it up. Yeah. How did you pivot?

**Aimee:** We, you know, it wasn't honestly it was what, a year that we probably had to pause quite a bit, but we just, we paused those events and then shifted them to a different time frame. And then there's so many, you know, we did some in barns or just more open spaces and we still did some and it felt like it was just a blur. I mean, it was just such a blip. And then we did some even at the Ritz with the masks on and which was a little odd.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. Right. And then it was people didn't even know nobody knew. Should we wear these? Should we not? When do we take them off? But yeah. Right. I didn't know. They were all asking me. I'm like right. So I don't know. You just go for it. And did you get all these bumps on your face too?

**Aimee:** Yes. Totally. I didn't wear a lot of mask actually. I was kind of I didn't either.

**Lindsay:** I only I had it in my pocket most of the time. I called it I would wear it on my chin.

**Aimee:** Me too. I was like okay. Yeah. I was like, it was down here, but I had it if I needed it. And then I switched to a bandana and I pulled that off because I could make that a fashion statement and I would have it with me in case somebody was like, "Hey, you got to cover your face."

**Lindsay:** I love it. I was like, "Okay." You're like me. And then I went on an airplane and then they were like, "Oh, no. They've banned that. You can't just wear a bandana anymore. You have to wear a mask."

**Aimee:** But did you even have one?

**Lindsay:** Well, they always had one.

**Aimee:** Yeah, they had. Yeah, that was when I was like, "Oh, man." And you couldn't breathe.

**Lindsay:** I couldn't breathe. No, I couldn't breathe. No. And I just don't know. That was such a weird time. But yeah, that's one of the reasons why the kids got homeschooled because we started homeschooling. Julian, my oldest, just is a little on the spectrum, a little atypical that name, by the way.

**Aimee:** Thank you.

**Lindsay:** And there was just no way I was going to make that kid wear a mask on his face. And I don't even think he could have, you know, I really don't think that that would have been a good something that could have been expected of him. And there's no way.

**Aimee:** So, well, I actually read that it which completely makes sense. It prohibits their learning, right? Because you can't read the lips and then you only see half of the face. And I, you know, I think that a lot of people read lips and you don't even know that you're doing it. So often times you can't even hear the person as you typically would.

**Lindsay:** Well, Indie was born in 2020 and I thought, how is this going to impede her social learning because we're working with basically she's a non-verbal human and so she all of the things she's learning about socialization are about facial expressions, tonality, and all these things. Nonverbals that are, you know, hiding behind a mask. I thought, wow. 

But, you know, at the same time, we did have a school with 10 kids at our house when she was born. So, I thought that probably saved her because she really hasn't had any learning loss or social deficits at all. She's good. She is a pistol.

**Aimee:** I bet she is. Yeah, she really is. I need to see a picture after this is over.

**Lindsay:** Oh my gosh. Yes. Absolutely. I'll share it with you. Oh my gosh. She's my mother. She's a clone of my mother. I don't know if you ever met my mom in high school, but she really is. She's got the curly blonde hair, the blue eyes.

**Aimee:** Does your mom have curly blonde hair and blue eyes?

**Lindsay:** Yes. I mean, she looks just like her. You got her features. You must look like your dad.

**Aimee:** I do. I look like my dad. Yeah, I do. I look like my dad. And then it's cute because Paul, he looks like me. My baby. My 2-year-old. My sweet because I named him after my brother because well when you said your brother I was like well of course your brother looks like you and Paul right but then okay that's your baby Paul.

**Lindsay:** Yeah I know everyone's like wait there's two Pauls I'm like yeah there's two Pauls.

**Aimee:** Cool I love that because you guys are so close and your brother Oh I always called my boys Paul on accident because my baby Paul my brother Paul was my first baby and so okay let's name this last one Paul and then I'll get his name right.

**Lindsay:** There you go. That's easy. Done. Right. Done. Easy. Cool. So easy.

**Aimee:** What kind of technology do you use to ease your workload with the BP Collective?

**Lindsay:** Right. Good question. You know, okay. Obviously, my business is creative, right? So, I still love Pinterest just because then you can get so many different ideas going and inspiration. Canva obviously is a big one now. Starting to get into the AI world quickly. That's not going anywhere, right? And there's so much to explore.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. So much. Yeah. What's your favorite? Are you playing with any AI Chat GPT or Grok or anybody like that?

**Lindsay:** Yeah, definitely Chat GPT. And I mean, it's fascinating to me. It's just fascinating and then it's crazy that they get to know you.

**Aimee:** Right. Right. Yeah. No, they really do. Yeah. It's so interesting, isn't it?

**Lindsay:** It's fascinating. Yes. So, I'm exploring that more and more, but yeah, and then obviously calendar is still a huge one. I think it always will be. So, back to basics, too. And I think that's kind of that fine line.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I feel like Canva for me is the next thing that I've got to really get into. That's, you know, because if you can use chat GPT and Canva together, have you been playing with it?

**Lindsay:** No. And I need to find the time. I don't ever say that you're, you know, you're never too busy for anything, but there's always, so I think I need to just schedule it on my calendar and really start to learn that a little bit more, but yeah, basically it's so cool because you can create basic little prompts that you can put into Canva and then it will instantly using AI generate the image that you're looking for.

**Aimee:** It's amazing. Really, really cool.

**Lindsay:** Yeah, I know. It's Yeah, I mean, it's moving so quickly right now. It is not quicker than me, but I'm trying to keep up with it.

**Aimee:** Good. Good. What about you? Are you Oh, yeah. I love both. I actually use chat and Grok and I use them. I'll often times generate something here, then bring it over there, and then take it here, and I'll bring it back over there. And, you know, I use it mostly when I'm doing my podcast. It helps me come up with good questions to ask them. It's just been a gift with all of the writing that I have to do in my business as well. Crafting emails to employees, even like how to comm. It's my HR assistant. It's fantastic. It's my legal assistant. 

I recently last week used it to help draft a legal document for my mom for the word mark on the manage her. And then my mom goes, "Who wrote this?" And I'm like, "Oh, Chat GPT wrote it." And she goes, "Wait, Chat GPT can write this?" I was like, "Yeah, mom." She's like, "Should I get it?" I'm like, "Yeah, yes. It's not going anywhere."

**Lindsay:** Yeah. So, she got Chat GPT so she can, you know, get a draft. Yes. At least get a draft for your legal practice and save yourself a little bit of time.
